one_line_takeaway: D2D defends LLMs against fine-tuning-based jailbreaking by rewriting ordinary training text to raise its perplexity and inject safety cues, so fine-tuning on it embeds robustness with zero inference-time overhead, pushing safety rates to ~100% (best when applied post-attack or at all stages).
key_finding: All-stage D2D drives the safety rate to essentially 100% while largely preserving usefulness: on Llama-3-8B under AOA it reaches 100% SR (safety score 4.93) and under ExH 99.2% SR (4.81), versus a NoDef baseline of only 15.2% SR (ExH) / 21.8% (AOA) on the same model. Among single-stage variants, post-attack defense is the strongest ('the latecomer outperforms early starters', e.g. 91.7-93.6% SR on Llama-3, 93.1-95.7% on Vicuna-13B), because the most recent fine-tuning dominates model behavior; pre-attack is the weakest single stage (~33-49% SR). The SafeData baseline reaches decent safety (78-87% SR) but visibly degrades usefulness. The ablation shows the seed set is the most load-bearing component (removing it drops ExH SR on Llama-3 from 99.2% to 52.6%), and the helpfulness score is what protects utility (removing it collapses S_HELP to ~1.18 and S_BERT to ~0.26 even though safety stays moderate). Perplexity analysis (Fig. 3) confirms the design intuition: after D2D the model shows lower perplexity on safe texts and higher perplexity on harmful ones, while general-domain perplexity is largely unchanged. A 'mutual reinforcement' effect holds, and only 10% curated data can offset 20% harmful data; beam depth beyond 5 iterations adds no gain.
core_contribution: The paper proposes D2D (Data to Defense), a data-curation framework that defends LLMs against fine-tuning-based (customization-time) jailbreaking WITHOUT adding any inference-time defensive module. Its own novel claim rests on a stated intuition that high perplexity signals novel knowledge to an LLM: D2D takes ordinary general-domain text and iteratively rewrites it to (a) increase its perplexity from the victim model's perspective while (b) infusing 'safety implications' (safety-aware keywords, responsible tone, benign semantics), so that fine-tuning on these curated texts embeds safety awareness into the model's weights. The curation uses a safety seed set, GPT-4o-driven output sampling (temperature + nucleus sampling) and a beam search that ranks candidates by perplexity subject to a helpfulness constraint (Algorithm 1). A second contribution is the lifecycle framing: the same curated data can be injected before customization (immunize), during customization (neutralize injected harmful samples), after customization (restore a compromised model), or at all stages combined. NOTE: the ExH and AOA jailbreak attacks the paper defends against, and the customization/robustification background, come from cited prior work (Qi et al. 2023; Yang et al. 2023; Dai et al. 2023), not from this paper's own contribution.
threat_model: Fine-tuning-based (customization-time) jailbreaking. A defender/service provider fine-tunes an aligned LLM theta on a crowdsourced dataset D. An adversary injects a harmful dataset D* = {Q*, R*} (security-focused queries paired with harmful responses) into D; training on D union D* compromises the model to theta*, which then emits harmful responses R_harm at inference when prompted with security-focused queries Q_sec (Eq. 1). The paper instantiates two attack styles from prior work: ExH (explicitly harmful, step-by-step malicious instructions) and AOA (Absolutely Obedient Agent prompts that make the model comply with any instruction). By default harmful examples are 10% of the fine-tuning set. The defender's goal is to keep the model producing safe/responsible responses to Q_sec while retaining usefulness on general-domain queries, and crucially without adding inference-time steps or external detection modules.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) scope is restricted to fine-tuning-/training-time jailbreaking; inference-time prompt-based jailbreaks (adversarial prompts) are explicitly out of scope and left to future work. (2) Experiments curate only general-domain texts; high-stakes domain-specific settings such as healthcare or cybersecurity are not evaluated, blamed on scarcity of public datasets, and left as future work. Reviewer-noted (grounded in the method): the whole pipeline depends heavily on GPT-4o both for curation and as the helpfulness/safety judge, so results are entangled with that single proprietary model; the default 'weakened' D2D curates only benign texts, so the reported numbers do not assume the defender can identify or touch the harmful samples; and safety is measured largely via GPT-4o-scored SR/S_SAFE, a model-graded metric rather than human red-team evaluation.

datasets_used: D_security: 2.5k security-domain evaluation samples combining AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023) and BeaverTails (Ji et al. 2024)
D_general: 15k general-domain evaluation samples drawn equally from Alpaca, BeaverTails, and Dolly
Seed set: 500+ safety keywords/phrases extracted via GraphRAG from 300+ AI/security papers, filtered by GPT-4o
quantitative_results: NoDef baseline (no defense): Llama-3-8B SR 15.2% (ExH) / 21.8% (AOA); Mistral-7B SR 11.7% (ExH) / 13.8% (AOA)
All-Stage D2D: Llama-3-8B SR 99.2% ExH (S_SAFE 4.81) and 100% AOA (S_SAFE 4.93); Vicuna-13B 98.3%/98.6%; Mistral-7B 96.5%/98.0%
Post-Attack single-stage D2D (best single stage): Llama-3-8B SR 91.7% ExH / 93.6% AOA; Vicuna-13B 93.1% / 95.7%; Mistral-7B 87.5% / 91.6%
Pre-Attack single-stage D2D (weakest single stage): Llama-3-8B SR 44.6% ExH / 48.5% AOA
In-Attack single-stage D2D: Llama-3-8B SR 83.6% ExH / 85.2% AOA
SafeData baseline: Llama-3-8B SR 82.7% ExH / 84.8% AOA but with reduced usefulness
Ablation w/o seed set: Llama-3-8B ExH SR falls to 52.6% (from 99.2%)
Ablation w/o helpfulness score: usefulness collapses to S_HELP 1.18 / S_BERT 0.26 on Llama-3-8B (ExH) despite ~68.7% SR
Default settings: harmful data 10% of fine-tuning set, curated data 5% (half the harmful volume); beam size k=3; ~5 beam iterations sufficient; 10% curated can offset 20% harmful
baselines_compared: SafeData (directly inject safety-focused samples into fine-tuning data)
RandDrop (randomly drop 20% or 50% of fine-tuning data; inspired by Zhang et al. 2023b)
PPLDrop (drop 20% or 50% of highest-perplexity fine-tuning data; inspired by Hu et al. 2023)
